era_context: The American Civil War was in its fourth year, with Union forces gaining the upper hand. European powers were consolidating nationalist movements and imperial ambitions. The Industrial Revolution was accelerating technological change. The Second Schleswig War was fought in Northern Europe, reshaping the balance of power in the region.

events.title: Second Schleswig War (German-Danish War)
events.description: The Second Schleswig War (also called the German-Danish War) was fought between Denmark and the German Confederation (primarily Prussia and Austria) from February to October 1864 over control of the duchies of Schleswig and Holstein. The war resulted in a decisive German victory and the Treaty of Vienna (October 30, 1864), which ceded Schleswig and Holstein to Prussia and Austria. The war was a crucial step in Prussian expansion and German unification, as it gave Prussia control of strategic territory and demonstrated Prussian military superiority. The war also displaced the Danish monarchy's influence in Northern Europe and contributed to the rise of Prussia as the dominant German power.
key_figures: General Helmuth von Moltke (Prussian Chief of Staff)

sources.name: Prussian military records and official state documents
Danish royal archives and military records
Treaty of Vienna (October 30, 1864)
Contemporary newspaper accounts (Times of London, German and Danish press)

events.certainty_note: The Second Schleswig War is extensively documented in military records, diplomatic correspondence, and contemporary accounts. Dates and outcomes are unambiguous.

events.title: Battle of the Wilderness and Overland Campaign (American Civil War)
events.description: General Ulysses S. Grant launched the Overland Campaign in Virginia in May 1864, beginning with the Battle of the Wilderness (May 5–7, 1864). This campaign marked a shift in Union strategy, with Grant pursuing a strategy of attrition against Confederate General Robert E. Lee's Army of Northern Virginia. The Overland Campaign included several major battles (Wilderness, Spotsylvania, Cold Harbor) and resulted in heavy casualties on both sides but gradually pushed Confederate forces back toward Richmond. The campaign demonstrated Grant's determination to pursue Lee relentlessly and marked the beginning of the end for the Confederacy. The campaign's brutality and high casualty rates shocked the American public but ultimately contributed to Union victory.
key_figures: General George Meade (Union Army of the Potomac commander)
General James H. Wilson (Union cavalry commander)

sources.name: Official Union Army records and Grant's memoirs
Confederate military records and Lee's correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts and soldier letters

events.certainty_note: The Overland Campaign is extensively documented in military records and contemporary accounts. Dates and outcomes are well-established.

events.title: Atlanta Campaign and fall of Atlanta (American Civil War)
events.description: General William Tecumseh Sherman led the Atlanta Campaign from May to September 1864, advancing through Georgia toward the city of Atlanta. The campaign included several major battles and culminated in the fall of Atlanta on September 2, 1864. Sherman's capture of Atlanta was a major Union victory that boosted Northern morale and contributed significantly to President Lincoln's re-election in November 1864. The campaign demonstrated Sherman's strategy of total war, involving the destruction of civilian infrastructure and resources to undermine Confederate war-making capacity. The fall of Atlanta marked a turning point in the Civil War, as it demonstrated that the Union could penetrate deep into Confederate territory and achieve major victories.
key_figures: General Joseph E. Johnston (Confederate commander, replaced by John Bell Hood)

sources.name: Official Union Army records and Sherman's memoirs
Confederate military records and correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts and soldier letters

events.certainty_note: The Atlanta Campaign is extensively documented in military records and contemporary accounts. The fall of Atlanta occurred on September 2, 1864.

events.title: Sherman's March to the Sea begins
events.description: Following the fall of Atlanta, General William Tecumseh Sherman began his famous March to the Sea in November 1864, advancing from Atlanta toward Savannah, Georgia. The march lasted from November 16 to December 21, 1864, and covered approximately 300 miles. Sherman's army employed a strategy of total war, destroying civilian infrastructure, crops, and resources to undermine Confederate war-making capacity and civilian morale. The march was controversial for its destruction of civilian property but was strategically significant in demonstrating Union military dominance and the vulnerability of the Confederate heartland. Sherman's army reached Savannah on December 21, 1864, and presented the city to President Lincoln as a Christmas gift.

sources.name: Official Union Army records and Sherman's memoirs
Confederate military records and correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts and soldier letters
Civilian accounts and diaries from Georgia

events.certainty_note: Sherman's March to the Sea is extensively documented in military records, memoirs, and contemporary accounts. Dates and route are well-established.

events.title: First Geneva Convention adopted
events.description: The First Geneva Convention for the Amelioration of the Condition of the Wounded and Sick in Armed Forces in the Field was adopted on August 22, 1864, at an international conference in Geneva, Switzerland. The convention was initiated by the International Committee of the Red Cross (founded in 1863) and was championed by Henry Dunant, a Swiss businessman who had witnessed the suffering of wounded soldiers at the Battle of Solferino in 1859. The convention established humanitarian principles for the treatment of wounded soldiers and established the Red Cross as a neutral organisation to provide medical care. The convention was signed by 12 nations and represented a major step toward international humanitarian law. The Geneva Conventions have since become the foundation of international humanitarian law and the laws of war.
key_figures: Henry Dunant (founder of the Red Cross)
Gustave Moynier (International Committee of the Red Cross)

sources.name: First Geneva Convention text and official conference records
International Committee of the Red Cross archives
Contemporary newspaper accounts and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The First Geneva Convention is documented in official conference records and the convention text. The date (August 22, 1864) is confirmed.
cross_references: 1863-001 (International Committee of the Red Cross founded)

events.title: Battle of the Crater (American Civil War)
events.description: The Battle of the Crater occurred on July 30, 1864, near Petersburg, Virginia, during the Siege of Petersburg. Union forces attempted to break through Confederate lines by detonating a large mine beneath Confederate fortifications, creating a crater. The explosion was successful, but the subsequent Union assault was poorly coordinated and repulsed by Confederate forces. The battle resulted in heavy Union casualties, including significant losses among the United States Colored Troops (USCT), who were among the first African American soldiers to see major combat in the Civil War. The battle is remembered for the heavy casualties and the controversial treatment of African American soldiers by Confederate forces.

sources.name: Official Union Army records and reports
Confederate military records and correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts and soldier letters
Accounts from United States Colored Troops soldiers

events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Crater is extensively documented in military records and contemporary accounts. The date (July 30, 1864) is confirmed.

events.title: Taiping Rebellion suppressed in China
events.description: The Taiping Rebellion, one of the deadliest conflicts of the 19th century, was finally suppressed in 1864 after more than a decade of warfare. The rebellion, led by Hong Xiuquan, who claimed to be the brother of Jesus Christ, had begun in 1851 and resulted in the deaths of millions of people. The final phase of the rebellion saw the fall of Nanjing (the Taiping capital) on June 1, 1864, to Qing imperial forces led by Zeng Guofan and Li Hongzhang. The suppression of the rebellion marked a significant victory for the Qing Dynasty and demonstrated the effectiveness of regional military commanders. However, the rebellion's suppression came at an enormous cost in lives and resources, and it weakened the Qing Dynasty's ability to resist foreign imperialism.
key_figures: Emperor Xianfeng of Qing (died 1861; succeeded by Tongzhi)

sources.name: Qing imperial records and military reports
Taiping records and documents
Western diplomatic reports from China
Contemporary Chinese and Western newspaper accounts

events.certainty_note: The suppression of the Taiping Rebellion is documented in Qing imperial records and Western diplomatic correspondence. The fall of Nanjing occurred on June 1, 1864.

events.title: International Working Men's Association (First International) founded
events.description: The International Working Men's Association, commonly known as the First International, was founded on September 28, 1864, at a meeting in St. Martin's Hall in London. The organisation was established to unite socialist and labour movements across Europe and promote workers' rights and international solidarity. The First International included various socialist factions, including Marxists, anarchists, and trade unionists. Karl Marx played a significant role in the organisation's development, drafting its constitution and leading its intellectual direction. The First International was influential in promoting socialist ideas and labour organising throughout the 1860s and 1870s, though it was eventually dissolved in 1876 due to internal conflicts.

sources.name: First International founding documents and constitution
Karl Marx's writings and correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts and labour publications

events.certainty_note: The First International's founding is documented in official founding documents and contemporary accounts. The date (September 28, 1864) is confirmed.

events.title: Maximilian becomes Emperor of Mexico
events.description: Archduke Maximilian of Austria was proclaimed Emperor of Mexico on June 10, 1864, with the support of French military forces. Maximilian had been invited to Mexico by Mexican conservatives and French Emperor Napoleon III, who sought to establish a European-style monarchy in Mexico and expand French influence in the Americas. Maximilian's reign was contested by Mexican republicans led by Benito Juárez, who continued to resist the foreign intervention. The Second French Intervention in Mexico (1862–1867) was controversial in Europe and the United States, and Maximilian's reign was ultimately short-lived. He was executed by Mexican republicans in 1867, marking the end of the French intervention and the restoration of the Mexican Republic.
key_figures: Archduke Maximilian of Austria (Emperor of Mexico)

sources.name: Mexican imperial records and government documents
French imperial records and diplomatic correspondence
Austrian imperial records
Contemporary newspaper accounts and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Maximilian's proclamation as Emperor is documented in Mexican imperial records and contemporary accounts. The date (June 10, 1864) is confirmed.

events.title: Lincoln re-elected as U.S. President
events.description: President Abraham Lincoln was re-elected on November 8, 1864, defeating General George B. McClellan, the Democratic candidate who opposed the war. Lincoln's re-election was significant because it demonstrated Northern support for continuing the war to achieve Union victory and the abolition of slavery. The election occurred at a crucial moment in the Civil War, when Union military victories (particularly the fall of Atlanta) had boosted Northern morale. Lincoln's re-election ensured that the war would continue until Confederate surrender and that the Thirteenth Amendment abolishing slavery would be pursued. The election was contested and controversial, with some Northern Democrats opposing the war and seeking a negotiated peace with the Confederacy.
key_figures: Vice President Andrew Johnson (re-elected with Lincoln)

sources.name: Official election records and results
Contemporary newspaper accounts and political journals
Lincoln's correspondence and speeches

events.certainty_note: Lincoln's re-election is documented in official election records and contemporary accounts. The date (November 8, 1864) is confirmed.
cross_references: 1864-003 (Fall of Atlanta, boosted Lincoln's re-election chances)

disconfirming_evidence: The Second Schleswig War is sometimes portrayed as a clear Prussian victory, but the subsequent joint administration of Schleswig and Holstein by Prussia and Austria created tensions that contributed to the Austro-Prussian War of 1866. Sherman's March to the Sea is often celebrated as a brilliant military strategy, but some historians have questioned whether the destruction of civilian property was militarily necessary or primarily served to terrorise the civilian population. The First International is sometimes portrayed as a unified socialist movement, but it was actually riven by internal conflicts between Marxists and anarchists from its founding, and these conflicts eventually led to its dissolution. The Taiping Rebellion's suppression is sometimes attributed primarily to Zeng Guofan and Li Hongzhang, but foreign military advisers and weapons also played a significant role in the final campaigns.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1864 is exceptionally rich for American and European events, with extensive documentation of the Civil War campaigns, the Second Schleswig War, and the founding of the First International. Western sources dominate the documentation, with particularly detailed coverage of American military campaigns. Chinese sources for the Taiping Rebellion's suppression are less abundant in English-language historiography, and Western accounts often emphasise the role of foreign military advisers. The First International's founding is well-documented in socialist and labour history sources. The proclamation of Maximilian as Emperor of Mexico is documented in Mexican, French, and Austrian sources, though Mexican republican perspectives are sometimes underrepresented. The year is well-documented overall, with the caveat that non-Western and non-elite perspectives are underrepresented in English-language sources.

graph_edges.note: Second Schleswig War victory gave Prussia control of Schleswig and Holstein, contributing to Prussian dominance that led to Austro-Prussian War
Overland Campaign and Atlanta Campaign were simultaneous Union offensives in 1864
Fall of Atlanta enabled Sherman's March to the Sea
Fall of Atlanta boosted Northern morale and contributed to Lincoln's re-election
First Geneva Convention established foundation for international humanitarian law
Taiping Rebellion suppression occurred during period of Qing Dynasty weakness due to Second Opium War
First International founded in 1864; dissolved in 1876 due to internal conflicts
Maximilian's proclamation as Emperor led to his execution in 1867
